title: Empirical observation of negligible fairness–accuracy trade-offs in machine learning for public policy
methods: random forest / logistic regression / gradient boosting / decision trees
data_sources: Johnson County KS administrative data (mental health, jail, police, ambulance) — DUA
San Jose CA Code Enforcement Office inspection records — DUA
El Salvador Ministry of Education student-level data — DUA

data_scope.time_period: data spanning multiple years per context; evaluation windows of 4 months (mental health) to 2 years (crowdfunding)

core_conclusion: Across four real-world public policy domains (inmate mental health, housing safety, student dropout, education crowdfunding), post-hoc disparity mitigation via subgroup-specific score thresholds substantially improved recall equity across racial, income, gender, and geographic subgroups without any statistically significant or practically meaningful loss of predictive accuracy (precision at top k). The fairness–accuracy trade-off, widely assumed from theoretical ML bounds, was empirically negligible in all settings, and robust across temporal cohorts, programme scale (list size k), and protected-group size.
why_it_matters: Directly falsifies the dominant practitioner assumption that equity requires sacrificing accuracy; demonstrates a simple, retraining-free mitigation method applicable to existing government ML pipelines; covers four high-stakes human-consequential policy domains; validated across 5–10 temporal cohorts per context and sensitivity-tested on group size and programme scale; fully open-source replication code provided.
why_nature: Overturns a received theoretical belief with systematic multi-domain empirical evidence — a conceptual correction rather than an incremental improvement; cross-domain generalization (four problem types, multiple countries, multiple demographic attributes) demonstrates breadth valued by Nature MI; addresses racial equity in criminal justice and social services within Nature MI's responsible-AI editorial remit; dual contribution of technical method (recall-equalizing thresholds + intertemporal validation) and direct practitioner guidance.
added_value_to_my_research: Recall-equalizing threshold logic → fairness-aware 2SFCA/R2SFCA that equalizes healthcare recall across demographic and geographic subgroups
Intertemporal cross-validation schema → temporally robust healthcare accessibility prediction in rapidly urbanizing Chinese cities
SHAP-based mechanism diagnosis → feature-level fairness attribution in spatial prediction models to identify geographic proxies of disparity
Negligible trade-off finding as prior → equity-constrained EV charging station and public facility siting without meaningful coverage sacrifice
Demographic recall-disparity audit → spatially and demographically fair crime/risk surface modeling using spatiotemporal KDE

related.title: Algorithmic fairness and bias mitigation for clinical machine learning with deep reinforcement learning
related.relation: complementary fairness-in-ML work with DRL-based in-training mitigation in healthcare, contrasts with this paper's simpler post-hoc approach

related.title: Event-level prediction of urban crime reveals a signature of enforcement bias in US cities
related.relation: extends the fairness audit to predictive policing where biased enforcement labels compound model bias — a harder test for negligible trade-off

related.title: Causal inference and counterfactual prediction in machine learning for actionable healthcare
related.relation: addresses causal/counterfactual grounding for policy ML predictions, providing the mechanistic complement to this paper's empirical fairness observations

related.title: A causal perspective on dataset bias in machine learning for medical imaging
related.relation: addresses dataset bias sources and causal debiasing in high-stakes ML, providing a mechanistic account of where disparities originate

figures.caption: Methods illustration and motivating results: (a) subgroup-specific threshold intuition, (b) intertemporal validation scheme, (c–d) precision vs. recall-disparity scatter before/after adjustment in inmate mental health context, (e) distribution of precision changes after mitigation.

Recall disparity vs. precision at top k across all four policy contexts comparing unmitigated and mitigated model selection strategies (education crowdfunding k=1,000; inmate mental health k=500; student outcomes k=10,000; housing safety k=500).
Temporal stability of disparity mitigation in inmate mental health (k=500): precision at top 500 over 10 cohorts; white vs. Black recall disparity; white vs. Hispanic recall disparity.

Sensitivity analysis in inmate mental health: disparity and precision vs. list size k (panels a–c); recall disparity vs. resampled Hispanic subgroup size from 5% to 38% (panel d), showing composite model degrades at small group sizes.
